name: Tiny Bradshaw
description: American jazz and rhythm and blues bandleader, singer, composer, pianist, and drummer (1907–1958)
abstract: Myron Carlton "Tiny" Bradshaw (September 23, 1907 – November 26, 1958) was an American jazz and rhythm and blues bandleader, composer, singer, pianist, and drummer. His biggest hit was "Well Oh Well" in 1950, and the following year he recorded "The Train Kept A-Rollin'", a song that was pivotal to the development of rock and roll.

about_quotes.speaker: Sam Jones
about_quotes.evidence: we want you to join the band--Tiny Bradshaw's band... Bradshaw told me--say 'look. You on the payroll as of right now.'

about_quotes.speaker: Norris Turney
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Gee by the mid 40s then you started playing with some fairly well known names: Tiny Bradshaw... [INTERVIEWEE] Oh yeah.
about_quotes.doc_title: Norris Turney interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: He certainly knew me because we had worked together in Luis Russell's orchestra at the Roseland

about_quotes.speaker: Russell Procope
about_quotes.evidence: by the time I joined his band and went to Chicago... I had a good time in Tiny's band

about_quotes.speaker: Leonard Phillips
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Then you played with Tiny Bradshaw in '53. [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah.

about_quotes.speaker: Jackie Williams
about_quotes.evidence: They’re playing for Tiny Bradshaw... So finally we got it all together — they got it together. So we’re on the way out to Jersey... we was playing at the Mosque... And I asked Tiny Bradshaw... You know Tiny and those guys, they were known for that strong beat.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jackie Williams interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995
